g.data-narration: This topic covers important runtime techniques for reducing energy consumption in computing systems including DVFS, power-aware scheduling, and compiler optimizations.
Runtime energy optimization adapts to changing workloads during real-time program execution, reducing power efficiently without compromising performance through dynamic techniques.
g.text: Runtime approaches adapt dynamically to workload changes during program execution, unlike static optimizations.
They operate at hardware, OS, compiler, and application levels to reduce energy consumption.

g.data-narration: DVFS adjusts processor voltage and frequency based on workload using the power formula P equals C V squared f, achieving significant energy savings.
g.text: Power Formula: P = C × V² × f — Voltage reduction has the greatest impact
OS manages P-states: Performance, Powersave, On-demand, Conservative governors

g.data-narration: Power-aware scheduling considers energy as a primary optimization metric alongside execution time, distributing workloads efficiently to maximize component idle states.
g.text: Key Techniques: Load balancing, Task consolidation, Energy-aware placement, Dynamic thread packing
Goal: Group I/O tasks together to allow CPU cores to enter low-power states earlier

g.data-narration: Compilers optimize energy through instruction selection, loop transformations, function inlining, and significant data layout improvements for significantly better cache utilization.
g.text: Instruction Selection: Use shifts instead of multiplication when possible
Loop Transformations: Improve cache locality | Data Layout: Minimize cache misses

g.data-narration: Energy-efficient algorithms minimize computational complexity and memory accesses since cache misses consume 100 to 300 times more energy than cache hits.
g.text: Lower algorithmic complexity = fewer operations = less energy consumed
Cache-friendly design: Cache misses cost 100-300x more energy than hits
Approximate computing trades accuracy for energy savings in error-tolerant applications

g.data-narration: Runtime power management extends beyond CPU to include device power states, memory power management, and thermal-aware task scheduling for overall system efficiency.
g.text: Device Power Management: Turn off unused peripherals, hard drives, network cards
Memory Power: Partial array self-refresh, dynamic retirement of memory banks
Thermal Management: Prevents throttling by monitoring temperature and adjusting performance
